preview_text:
The model favours Egypt to win, assigning them a 47% chance of victory. New Zealand has a 23% probability of securing three points, a gap of 24 percentage points that reflects material separation in the forecast.

The expected scoreline distribution shows a meaningful spread. A draw at 30% represents a plausible outcome, sitting well above the lower tail of either team's win probability. The win probabilities alone suggest a competitive fixture, but the draw probability indicates neither favourite nor underdog dominates decisively; Egypt enters with advantage, yet the match carries genuine uncertainty.

The model and sportsbook consensus align closely in direction and magnitude. The market prices Egypt slightly higher (54.5% vs. 47%) and correspondingly lowers New Zealand (19.7% vs. 23%) by similar margins. These gaps—roughly 5 percentage points in each direction—sit at the threshold of material divergence. Polymarket prices track the sportsbook consensus tightly, suggesting unified market conviction around an Egypt lean.
